content.text: Azure allows you to move resources between resource groups and across subscriptions without deleting and recreating them. The move operation transfers the ARM metadata—the resource's registration in Azure—to the new scope. The underlying data (VM disks, storage blobs, database rows) stays in place throughout the move.

The resource ID changes after a move. Any reference to the resource by its full ARM resource ID—in Terraform state, application configuration, Azure Policy exemptions, role assignments to the old path—must be updated.

attrs.alt: ARM resource move flow: source RG to target RG via ARM Move Engine with validation, locking, and ID update

content.text: Not every resource type supports the move operation. Azure documents a support matrix, but the authoritative check is to run the validation API call against your specific resources before executing any move.
Must stop VM first for disk consistency
Blob data stays, only metadata moves
Move server; databases move with it
Delete and recreate in new RG
Rule of thumb: always run validateMoveResources before executing a move. The validation API checks your specific resource combination — the support matrix is necessary but not sufficient.

The validateMoveResources action is a dry run. ARM checks whether the resource types support the move, whether cross-subscription constraints are met, and whether any dependencies would be broken. It returns 204 No Content on success or a detailed error body on failure.

# Validate a move before executing it — always do this first
SOURCE_RG=myapp-old-rg
TARGET_RG=myapp-new-rg
SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Get resource IDs to move
RESOURCE_IDS=$(az resource list \
  --resource-group $SOURCE_RG \
  --query '[].id' --output tsv | tr '\n' ',')

# Strip trailing comma
RESOURCE_IDS=${RESOURCE_IDS%,}

# Validate (returns 204 if OK, error body if not)
az resource invoke-action \
  --action validateMoveResources \
  --ids /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/$SOURCE_RG \
  --request-body "{
    \"resources\": [\"$RESOURCE_IDS\"],
    \"targetResourceGroup\": \"/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/$TARGET_RG\"
  }"
The validation is asynchronous—ARM returns 202 Accepted and you poll the Location header. The Azure CLI handles this automatically and blocks until the result is available.
Pass one or more resource IDs to az resource move. All listed resources move in a single atomic operation—either all succeed or all are rolled back.

# Execute the move (only after validation passes)
az resource move \
  --destination-group myapp-new-rg \
  --ids /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-old-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm \
       /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-old-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorageacct \
       /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-old-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault

# For cross-subscription moves, add --destination-subscriptionid
az resource move \
  --destination-group prod-rg \
  --destination-subscriptionid <prod-subscription-id> \
  --ids /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/staging-rg/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/mysqlserver

# PowerShell: move resources between resource groups
$sourceRg = 'myapp-old-rg'
$targetRg = 'myapp-new-rg'
$targetSubId = (Get-AzContext).Subscription.Id

# Get all resources to move (exclude non-movable types)
$resources = Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName $sourceRg |
  Where-Object { $_.ResourceType -notmatch 'applicationGateways/v1' }

# Validate
Test-AzResourceGroupMoveResource `
  -SourceResourceGroupName $sourceRg `
  -DestinationResourceGroupName $targetRg `
  -ResourceId ($resources.ResourceId)

# Move
Move-AzResource `
  -ResourceId ($resources.ResourceId) `
  -DestinationResourceGroupName $targetRg `
  -DestinationSubscriptionId $targetSubId `
  -Force
The Python SDK uses begin_validate_move_resources and begin_move_resources, both returning long-running operation pollers.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.resource.resources.models import ResourcesMoveInfo

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

# List all resources in source group
source_resources = [
    r.id for r in client.resources.list_by_resource_group('myapp-old-rg')
]

# Validate move
target_rg = f'/subscriptions/{subscription_id}/resourceGroups/myapp-new-rg'
move_info = ResourcesMoveInfo(
    resources=source_resources,
    target_resource_group=target_rg
)

# validate_move_resources returns a long-running operation
poller = client.resources.begin_validate_move_resources('myapp-old-rg', move_info)
poller.result()  # raises if validation fails
print('Validation passed — safe to move')

# Execute move
move_poller = client.resources.begin_move_resources('myapp-old-rg', move_info)
move_poller.result()
print('Move complete')

Cross-subscription moves follow the same API path but require that both the source and target subscription are registered with the same resource providers. The target subscription must also satisfy any Azure Policy constraints that would apply to the resources being moved.
Both subscriptions must be in the same Azure Active Directory tenant
The caller must have Microsoft.Resources/moveResources/action permission on the source RG and write access on the target RG
Resource provider registration: az provider register --namespace <namespace> in the target subscription
Policy compliance: the moved resources must satisfy policies in the target subscription
Some resource types (App Service, API Management) require same-region cross-sub moves
For resource types that do not support cross-subscription moves, the standard workaround is to export the ARM template from the source and redeploy it in the target subscription.

Key Vaults move successfully, but any access policies that reference the vault's resource ID (rather than the vault's URI) must be reapplied. RBAC-based access policies using the vault's URI are unaffected—role assignments follow the principal, not the resource path.

# Key Vault move requires updating the vault's tenant/subscription metadata
# After the move, the vault's soft-delete entries still reference the old RG

# Move the vault
az resource move \
  --destination-group myapp-new-rg \
  --ids /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-old-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault

# Verify the new resource ID
az keyvault show --name myvault --resource-group myapp-new-rg --query id -o tsv

# Update any service principals / MSI access policies that referenced the old resource ID
az keyvault set-policy \
  --name myvault \
  --resource-group myapp-new-rg \
  --object-id <managed-identity-object-id> \
  --secret-permissions get list

# If using RBAC (not access policies), role assignments persist automatically —
# they are tied to the principal, not the resource path
VNets can move as long as they have no attached gateways or Bastion hosts. NSGs and route tables attached to subnets move automatically when you include the parent VNet in the move operation.

# Virtual Networks CAN be moved if they have no:
#   - VPN Gateway
#   - Application Gateway
#   - Azure Bastion
#   - ExpressRoute circuit connection

# Check what's in the VNet before moving
az network vnet show \
  --name myvnet \
  --resource-group myapp-old-rg \
  --query '{subnets:subnets[].name, gatewayConnections:virtualNetworkPeerings[].name}'

# If safe, move the VNet and its subnets together
az resource move \
  --destination-group myapp-new-rg \
  --ids /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-old-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myvnet

# Subnets are child resources and move automatically with the parent
# NSGs and Route Tables attached to subnets also move if included in the same operation

After a move completes, verify that resources in the new group are healthy and that no references to the old resource group remain in the moved resources' configurations. Resource IDs embedded in properties (linked resource URIs, diagnostic setting storage account IDs, etc.) may point to the old path.
Update Terraform / ARM template state file
State still references old resource IDs
Update application configuration with new resource IDs
SDK clients constructed from resource ID will fail
Re-apply RBAC role assignments if they were on the old RG
RG-level assignments do not follow moved resources
Update diagnostic settings (storage account / Log Analytics IDs)
May still point to old RG path
Catch integration points that didn't update
Terraform State Management After a Move

When Azure resources are moved using az resource move (rather than Terraform), Terraform loses track of the resources — its state file still references the old resource group. There are two approaches to reconcile state.
The import approach works for any Terraform version. Remove the resource from state at the old path and re-import it at the new resource ID. Then update the resource_group_name in the .tf file.

# After a resource move, Terraform state references the old resource ID.
# Option 1: terraform import — re-point state to the new resource ID

# 1. Remove the old resource from state
terraform state rm azurerm_virtual_machine.myvm

# 2. Import the resource at its new resource ID
terraform import azurerm_virtual_machine.myvm \
  /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-new-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm

# 3. Update the resource_group_name in your .tf file
#    resource "azurerm_virtual_machine" "myvm" {
#      resource_group_name = "myapp-new-rg"   # was myapp-old-rg
#      ...
#    }

# 4. Plan to confirm no unintended changes
terraform plan

The moved block is cleaner for refactoring within a Terraform codebase — when the Terraform resource address changes but the underlying Azure resource does not. This does not help if the Azure resource was moved outside of Terraform's control.

# Option 2: terraform moved block (Terraform >= 1.1)
# Use when the resource group name changed in your module hierarchy

moved {
  from = azurerm_resource_group.old
  to   = azurerm_resource_group.new
}

# For resources inside the old group:
moved {
  from = module.old_rg.azurerm_virtual_machine.main
  to   = module.new_rg.azurerm_virtual_machine.main
}

# Run plan — Terraform confirms the rename without destroying/recreating
terraform plan
# Output: azurerm_virtual_machine.main has moved to module.new_rg.azurerm_virtual_machine.main
Azure resource moved via CLI, Terraform unaware
Renamed Terraform resource block, same Azure resource
Resource moved to different module in same workspace
terraform state rm + terraform import in correct workspace
For moving many resources at once, a shell script that excludes known non-movable types prevents the entire operation from failing when it encounters one unsupported resource.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Batch move all movable resources from one RG to another
# Skips known non-movable types before sending to ARM

SOURCE_RG="${1:-myapp-old-rg}"
TARGET_RG="${2:-myapp-new-rg}"
SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Non-movable resource types (extend this list as needed)
SKIP_TYPES=(
  'microsoft.network/applicationgateways'
  'microsoft.network/bastionhosts'
  'microsoft.recoveryservices/vaults'
)

# Build jq filter to exclude non-movable types
FILTER=$(printf ' and .type != "%s"' "${SKIP_TYPES[@]}" | sed 's/^ and //')

# Get movable resource IDs
RESOURCE_IDS=$(az resource list \
  --resource-group "$SOURCE_RG" \
  --query "[?$FILTER].id" -o tsv)

if [ -z "$RESOURCE_IDS" ]; then
  echo 'No movable resources found.'
  exit 0
fi

echo "Moving resources from $SOURCE_RG to $TARGET_RG..."
az resource move \
  --destination-group "$TARGET_RG" \
  --ids $RESOURCE_IDS

echo 'Move complete. Verify with:'
echo "  az resource list --resource-group $TARGET_RG -o table"
Tip: run the script with --what-if (dry-run) mode first by substituting the az resource move call with an az resource invoke-action validateMoveResources call on the same IDs.

ARM applies a temporary system lock to both the source and target resource groups during a move. Operations that require write access to either group will fail with a ScopeLocked error until the move completes. CanNotDelete locks on the RG do not block a move; ReadOnly locks do.

# During a move, ARM places a temporary read-only lock on both RGs.
# Any concurrent write operations will fail with:
# Error: ScopeLocked — The scope is locked for move.

# Check for existing locks that might conflict with a move
az lock list --resource-group myapp-old-rg -o table

# CanNotDelete locks do NOT block moves — moves are not deletes
# ReadOnly locks DO block moves — remove them before moving
az lock delete \
  --name myapp-readonly-lock \
  --resource-group myapp-old-rg

# After the move, re-apply the lock on the target RG
az lock create \
  --name myapp-lock \
  --resource-group myapp-new-rg \
  --lock-type ReadOnly
Move operations are long-running and appear in the Azure Activity Log under the operationName moveResources. You can poll the operation status or watch the Activity Log for completion.

# Monitor a move operation in the Activity Log
# Move operations appear as 'moveResources' events

az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group myapp-old-rg \
  --start-time 2024-06-01T00:00:00Z \
  --query "[?operationName.value == 'Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups/moveResources/action']
           .{time:eventTimestamp, status:status.value, caller:caller}" \
  -o table

# For in-flight moves, find the operation ID and poll status
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/operationresults/<opId>?api-version=2021-04-01"

# You can also get the move status from the target RG's operations list
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-new-rg?api-version=2022-09-01" \
  --query 'properties.provisioningState'
Role Assignments and Diagnostic Settings After a Move
Role assignments at the resource group level do not follow resources when they move. Assignments applied directly to individual resources (not the parent RG) do follow the resource.

# Role assignments on the SOURCE resource group do NOT move with resources.
# Assignments on INDIVIDUAL RESOURCES do move with those resources.

# Before the move: list role assignments on the source RG
az role assignment list \
  --resource-group myapp-old-rg \
  --query '[].{principalName:principalName, role:roleDefinitionName, scope:scope}' \
  -o table

# After the move: re-create necessary assignments on the target RG
az role assignment create \
  --assignee user@example.com \
  --role Contributor \
  --resource-group myapp-new-rg

# Role assignments on individual resources (not the RG) move automatically
# Verify with:
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-new-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm

Diagnostic settings are stored as child resources of the resource they monitor. When the parent resource moves, its diagnostics settings do not move — they must be exported from the source and re-created on the new resource ID.

# Diagnostic settings are NOT moved — they must be re-created in the target RG.
# Step 1: Export existing diagnostic settings from the source resource

RESOURCE_ID="/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-old-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm"

az monitor diagnostic-settings list \
  --resource $RESOURCE_ID \
  -o json > diag-settings-backup.json

# Step 2: After the move, re-create on the new resource ID
NEW_RESOURCE_ID="/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-new-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm"
LOG_ANALYTICS_ID="/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/monitoring-rg/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/myworkspace"

az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource $NEW_RESOURCE_ID \
  --name vm-diagnostics \
  --workspace $LOG_ANALYTICS_ID \
  --metrics '[{"category":"AllMetrics","enabled":true}]' \
  --logs '[{"category":"Administrative","enabled":true}]'
None — tags are part of the resource
Export and re-create on new resource ID
Delete and recreate in target RG
Azure Policy exemptions by resource ID
Update exemption with new resource ID
Re-create lock on target RG after move

Moving Azure resources between resource groups transfers ARM metadata without touching the underlying data. Key points: validate first with validateMoveResources, expect resource IDs to change, ensure role assignments and application configs are updated, and check the support matrix—some resource types (App Gateway v1, Azure Bastion) cannot move. Cross-subscription moves add tenant, policy, and provider-registration constraints.
